original_abstract: It is now well known that the primary interactions of biological entities (e.g., tissues and cells) with nanoparticles (NPs) are strongly influenced by the protein composition of the "corona" (i.e., the NP surface attached proteins). The composition of the corona strongly depends on the protein source (e.g., human plasma). Because the protein source determines the NP corona, it is reasonable to hypothesize that humans with specific disease(s) may have specific NP coronas. To test this hypothesis, we incubated two different hydrophobic/hydrophilic types of NPs (polystyrene and silica) with plasma from human subjects with different diseases and medical conditions (e.g., breast cancer, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, rheumatism, fauvism, smoking, hemodialysis, thalassemia, hemophilia A and B, pregnancy, common cold and hypofibrinogenemia). Our results demonstrate that the type of disease has a crucial role in the protein composition of the NP corona. Based on these results, we introduce the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a determinant factor in nano-biomedical science. This study will help researchers rationally design experiments based on the "personalized protein corona" for clinical and biological applications.
humanized_abstract: The impact of nanoparticles (NPs) on biological organisms is becoming a huge concern in nanotechnology in recent years. Bioactivities of nanoparticles are believed to occur via interactions with cells and tissues, and at the most basic level, these interactions are dictated by protein adsorption on nanoparticles surfaces, commonly referred as the corona of NPs. The protein corona of nanoparticles is proven to be highly dependent on the composition of protein solutions used in the studies. Because some humans are naturally affected by various diseases in their life time, we thus infer that there may have unique protein corona for nanoparticles interacting with diseases affected human plasma samples. Here, we have studied how plasma samples from a variety of human diseases such as breast cancer, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, rheumatism, fauvism, smoker, hemodialysis patients, thalassemia, and hemophilia A and B, pregnancy, common cold and hypofibrinogenemia, etc interact with two hydrophobic/hydrophilic different composition of nanoparticles such as polystyrene (PS) and silica NPs, and confirm that the nature of diseases in fact influence strongly the final protein corona formed. We for the first time reported a new term in nanoscience, termed as "personalized protein corona" of NPs and believe that the basic findings and understanding provided in this work will contribute to help researchers doing experimental works and designing their experiments accordingly to be applicable and beneficial to clinical and biomedical studies.
